coaching.coaching: Realign E2+E3 gate requirement from parent epic to the parent's plan — the parent wins on conflict; if the parent is genuinely wrong, update the PARENT first (which forces its re-review), never silently diverge the leaf.

coaching.coaching: Plan how agentic grounding step fallback behaviour will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.

coaching.coaching: Research prior art / OSS for removal-rationale registry membership precedent before building it custom.

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind observability gap for agentic finder failures first.

coaching.coaching: Research prior art / OSS for removal-rationale eval-spec and artifact precedent before building it custom.
coaching.subject: removal-rationale eval-spec and artifact precedent

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the E2+E3 gating conflict: either (a) update the parent epic to explicitly accept the bounded sanity check as R1's eval gate in lieu of E2+E3 (and document why cost rules out E2/E3 for this story specifically), or (b) restore E2+E3 as a leaf dependency and align the Scope accordingly — the leaf may not eliminate a parent-required gate without first updating the parent.
evidence: Leaf Scope: 'OUT: any E2/E3 batch eval (permanently rejected on cost)'
Parent epic integration record: 'R1=59b7 (capability probe, gated E2+E3)'

Parent epic AC: 'Each recommendation child (R1-R7) is either landed through Gerrit with its own AC gate terms demonstrably passed... no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate'
Leaf title: 'R1: asserted-capability grounding probe (G6/E4 rubric family, gated on E2+E3)' — the title declares the gate the Scope then eliminates
Leaf AC for sanity check: 'NO E2/E3 corpus, NO batch eval' — the only eval gate is >=2/3 recall on 3 reconstructed cases
findings.finding: The leaf eliminates the E2+E3 eval gate that the parent epic explicitly requires for R1, substituting a self-defined bounded sanity check (recall≥0.66 on 3 cases, ≤8 live runs). The parent's AC states 'no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate' and the integration record explicitly designates R1 as 'gated E2+E3'. The leaf's Scope declares E2/E3 'permanently rejected on cost' — contradicting the parent's gating requirement without updating the parent first.
findings.location: Leaf Scope section ('OUT: any E2/E3 batch eval (permanently rejected on cost)') vs. Parent epic 6982 AC ('no detection-bearing recommendation implemented without its eval gate') and integration record ('R1=59b7 (capability probe, gated E2+E3)')
scenarios: An implementer follows the leaf plan, passes the bounded sanity check, and ships the criterion without E2+E3 passage — violating the parent's 'no detection-bearing recommendation without its eval gate' AC.
A reviewer approving the leaf's Gerrit change discovers the parent AC is unsatisfied and blocks merge, creating a mid-implementation conflict.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit comment to the parent epic 6982 recording the operator decision to accept the bounded sanity check (recall≥0.66 on 3 E1-verified cases) as R1's eval gate in lieu of E2+E3 batch eval, citing the cost constraint. Then update the leaf's Scope from 'permanently rejected on cost' to 'replaced by bounded sanity check per operator direction [date]' and remove 'gated on E2+E3' from the leaf title or replace it with 'gated on bounded sanity check (E2/E3 cost-rejected)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback/ABSTAIN rule for the agentic grounding step when the named module/symbol is unlocatable (e.g., 'if Grep/Read/resolve_symbol cannot locate the named module, ABSTAIN-with-coverage rather than fire — a module not yet created is not a refuted capability claim').
evidence: Plan text: 'The finder greps/reads the repo (and resolve_symbol for installed deps) to confirm the module's actual capability surface'
E4 rubric (plan_review_E4.md): 'ABSTAIN-with-coverage on an external-fact exclusion the tools cannot settle'
removal-rationale.md lines 48-51: 'ACCEPTED LIMITATION (log in coverage, do not hide): a purely-latent guard whose removal changes behavior only for inputs never exercised today...'
ABSENCE: no fallback or ABSTAIN-with-coverage rule in the plan for when resolve_symbol returns UNRESOLVED or Grep finds no module
findings.finding: The agentic grounding step ('greps/reads the repo (and `resolve_symbol` for installed deps) to confirm the module's actual capability surface') has no specified fallback for when the named module/symbol is unlocatable — e.g., the module doesn't exist yet (greenfield plan), or `resolve_symbol` returns UNRESOLVED. The E4 rubric specifies 'ABSTAIN-with-coverage on an external-fact exclusion the tools cannot settle'; `removal-rationale` specifies its fail-open accepted limitation. Without an equivalent rule, the agentic finder has no instruction on whether to fire, abstain, or escalate when the grounding tool call is inconclusive.
findings.location: ## What — 'The finder greps/reads the repo (and `resolve_symbol` for installed deps) to confirm the module's actual capability surface'
scenarios: The plan under review names a module that has not yet been created (it is a deliverable of the plan itself); the agentic finder cannot locate it and must decide whether to fire or abstain.
resolve_symbol returns UNRESOLVED for an optional installed dependency; without a rule, the finder may fabricate a gap or silently pass.

evidence: vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the two criterion-local binary sub-answer keys (names + `{yes|no|insufficient}` vocabulary) for `asserted-capability` in the plan's ## What section, matching the `removal-rationale` precedent pattern (e.g., `capability_claimed` as the gate sub-answer, `claim_refuted_by_code` as the consequential sub-answer).
evidence: Plan text: 'Pass-2 verifier grades its two criterion-local binary sub-answers'

removal-rationale.md defines: 'CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS (criterion-local): - removes_external_behavior_or_guarded_fence {yes|no|insufficient} — the GATE ... - removal_scenario_grounded {yes|no|insufficient}'
criteria_routing.json for removal-rationale has checklist keys 'removes_external_behavior_or_guarded_fence' and 'removal_scenario_grounded' matching the rubric
ABSENCE: no equivalent named sub-answer keys or {yes|no|insufficient} vocabulary appears anywhere in the plan for asserted-capability
findings.finding: The two criterion-local binary sub-answer keys and their value vocabulary for `asserted-capability` are never defined in the plan. The cited structural precedent (`removal-rationale`) defines both named keys and the `{yes|no|insufficient}` vocabulary explicitly in its rubric file; the plan says 'Pass-2 verifier grades its two criterion-local binary sub-answers' but names neither sub-answer key (e.g., something like `capability_surface_asserted` / `assertion_refuted_by_code`) nor confirms their closed vocabulary, leaving the rubric author and the Pass-2 verifier prompt without a contract.
findings.location: ## What — 'Pass-2 verifier grades its two criterion-local binary sub-answers'; cf. plan_review_removal_rationale.md lines 40-46
scenarios: A rubric author implements the rubric file without knowing the sub-answer key names, producing a prompt that doesn't match what the checklist_expect in eval fixtures expects.
The deterministic unit test AC ('prompt-contract sub-answer keys') cannot be written without knowing what keys to assert, leaving the AC underspecified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ## What: 'CHECKLIST SUB-ANSWERS (criterion-local, matching removal-rationale precedent): `capability_surface_claimed {yes|no|insufficient}` — the GATE: does the plan assert a named module/symbol provides (or lacks) a specific capability it relies on? `no` → not-applicable → PASS. `claim_refuted_by_code {yes|no|insufficient}` — only when gated in: does a Grep/Read/resolve_symbol probe refute the asserted capability (direction A: module lacks C; direction B: C already exists when plan says absent)? `no` → PASS.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Anchor AC6's grep pattern to confirm the authoritative posture declaration, e.g., `grep -n 'posture.*advisory\|ships advisory\|default_posture.*advisory'` on the rubric, or assert against the structured `criteria_routing.json` entry instead.
evidence: AC6 proving command: 'Proof: `grep -n "advisory"` on the rubric shows the advisory posture note.'
A rubric sentence like 'This criterion does NOT ship as advisory in future versions' would cause grep to exit 0, falsely passing the criterion.
Defect taxonomy item 1: substring false-positive — anchor the pattern with a word-boundary or quoted key.
findings.finding: AC6's proving command `grep -n "advisory"` is an unanchored substring match that will produce a passing exit-code on any occurrence of the string 'advisory' in the rubric file — including negations like 'not advisory', 'non-advisory', or comments — and does not confirm that the advisory posture is actually set as an authoritative declaration.
scenarios: Rubric file contains a sentence explaining why the criterion is NOT advisory in some edge case; grep exits 0 and the AC is incorrectly marked done.
A templated rubric header always contains the word 'advisory' in a disclaimer; the actual posture field is absent.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace AC6's grep with: `grep -nE '(posture|ships).*advisory' src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_asserted_capability.md` — or, more robustly, rely solely on the `criteria_routing.json` assertion already present in AC1 (`e['default_posture']=='advisory'`), which is a structured check, and drop the redundant fragile grep.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `asserted-capability` should be in `CODEBASE_GROUNDED` (triggering agentic Pass-2) or follow `removal-rationale`'s pattern of omission from that set; document the rationale either way, since both are AGENT-tier codebase-grounding advisory criteria.
evidence: Plan: 'added to registry.CANONICAL_LLM (registry.py:79), CODEBASE_GROUNDED (:66), and AGENT_TIER (:72)'
registry.py:66: CODEBASE_GROUNDED = frozenset({'E4', 'G1G2', 'A1', 'G6'}) — removal-rationale absent
registry.py:72-74: AGENT_TIER = frozenset({'G1G2', 'E4', 'A1', 'G6', ...}) — removal-rationale absent despite exec:AGENT in its routing entry
workflow_ops.py:278-303: CODEBASE_GROUNDED drives agentic-vs-single-turn Pass-2 verifier branch — adding asserted-capability here increases Pass-2 cost on every firing
criteria_routing.json:1138-1161: removal-rationale has exec:AGENT and facet:codebase-grounding but was deliberately excluded from both sets
Plan's 'direct structural precedent' claim: 'removal-rationale (the removal-side code-grounded advisory dual of A1) is the direct structural precedent'
findings.finding: The plan mandates adding `asserted-capability` to both `CODEBASE_GROUNDED` and `AGENT_TIER`, but `removal-rationale` — the plan's own 'direct structural precedent' for a gated-then-grounded advisory criterion — is deliberately absent from both sets in registry.py:66 and registry.py:72-74. `CODEBASE_GROUNDED` membership has a concrete behavioral consequence: any finding tagged with a `CODEBASE_GROUNDED` criterion triggers the expensive agentic Pass-2 verifier instead of the single-turn verifier (workflow_ops.py:278-303). Adding `asserted-capability` to `CODEBASE_GROUNDED` (but not `removal-rationale`) creates an unexplained cost asymmetry vs. the precedent without rationale.
findings.location: ## What — 'Registration seam' paragraph / AC line 1 proof snippet
scenarios: Any plan that fires `asserted-capability` makes the entire Pass-2 run in agentic mode, adding ~85× call cost vs. single-turn, when `removal-rationale` (same pattern, same tier) does not have this effect — the author may be surprised.

If the intent IS to add asserted-capability to CODEBASE_GROUNDED, the plan should also explain why removal-rationale was not added — else a future addition of removal-rationale to CODEBASE_GROUNDED will be missed.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the two binary sub-answer key names in the plan (e.g., `capability_present_asserted_absent` and `capability_absent_asserted_present`) so the rubric prompt and unit test have a shared, auditable contract.
evidence: Plan says: 'Pass-2 verifier grades its two criterion-local binary sub-answers' but never names the keys.
AC3 says unit tests cover 'prompt-contract sub-answer keys' — the keys to test are unknown until the executor decides them.
The rubric prompt (reviewers/plan_review_asserted_capability.md) and the unit test must agree on these names, but neither is constrained by the plan.
findings.finding: The two binary sub-answer key names for the pass-2 verifier are never specified in the plan. AC3 promises a unit test for 'prompt-contract sub-answer keys', but an executor must invent the key names (e.g., `capability_present_when_plan_says_absent`, `capability_absent_when_plan_asserts_present`) with no stated default — creating an ambiguity between the rubric prompt and the unit test.
findings.location: ## What — 'Pass-2 verifier grades its two criterion-local binary sub-answers'; AC3 — 'prompt-contract sub-answer keys'
scenarios: Executor writes a rubric prompt with keys A/B; unit test independently asserts keys C/D; both pass individually but the contract is inconsistent.
A future plan-review engineer reading only the plan cannot reproduce the expected schema.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the ## What section: 'The rubric prompt's structured response must include two boolean keys: `(A) capability_present_asserted_absent` (fires when module M lacks capability C that plan asserts it has) and `(B) capability_absent_asserted_present` (fires when module M already provides C that plan says must be built). Both keys are required by the pass-2 verifier and asserted in unit tests.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a co-located discrimination protocol to the ## What section specifying whether direction-A and direction-B share one gate+consequential sub-answer pair or require per-direction sub-answers, and whether scoring or the grading rule differs between the two directions.
evidence: Plan text: '(A) plan asserts named module M PROVIDES capability C, but M lacks C (dc58, db7b); (B) plan asserts capability C is ABSENT / must be built, but M already provides C (5886)'
Plan text: 'Two symmetric directions (one concern)'
ABSENCE: no protocol specified for whether the verifier answers one sub-question covering both directions, or two direction-specific sub-questions
removal-rationale.md: one gate sub-answer + one consequential sub-answer — no per-direction split needed because there is only one direction
findings.finding: The plan defines two 'symmetric directions' (A: module asserted to PROVIDE capability C but lacks it; B: capability C asserted ABSENT but already present) but provides no discrimination protocol co-located with the sub-answer schema specifying how the verifier distinguishes direction-A from direction-B — specifically, whether they share one gate sub-answer or require separate per-direction answers, and whether the scoring/impact differs by direction. The removal-rationale precedent has a single gate + single consequential sub-answer; an asymmetric two-direction criterion without a discrimination rule leaves the rubric author to invent the protocol.
findings.location: ## What — 'Two symmetric directions (one concern — capability claims about named modules must be grounded): (A)... (B)...'
scenarios: The rubric author writes separate sub-answers per direction (4 sub-answers), but the plan AC says 'two criterion-local binary sub-answers' — a contradiction that surfaces only during implementation.
The Pass-2 verifier prompt handles direction A and direction B inconsistently because the discrimination rule was never specified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement (log entry, metric counter, or error-state annotation) for agentic-finder infrastructure failures (LLM error, grep failure, `resolve_symbol` exception) so operators can distinguish 'criterion fired and found nothing' from 'criterion failed to run'.
evidence: Plan states 'advisory posture (never blocks)' but no mention of surfacing agentic-finder errors to operators
No AC requires a log entry, metric, or error state when the finder fails
Existing ACs only verify happy-path firing (>=2/3 misses) and structural registration — not error propagation
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new AGENT-tier agentic finder that performs LLM calls and file I/O (grep, `resolve_symbol`), but specifies no observability instrumentation — no metric, log, or alert — for when the finder itself fails (LLM timeout, grep error, `resolve_symbol` exception). Because the criterion ships advisory (never blocks), a silently failing finder will look identical to 'no capability-assertion found' in the review output, leaving operators unable to distinguish criterion failure from a genuinely clean plan.
findings.location: ## What — Mechanism paragraph; Acceptance Criteria (no AC covers finder-failure observability)
scenarios: LLM provider is rate-limited or times out during a plan review: the criterion silently emits no finding, operators see nothing wrong
A future repo restructure breaks the grep path: the criterion quietly stops firing, false-accept rate rises invisibly
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC: 'When the agentic finder raises an exception or the LLM call fails, the criterion emits a structured log entry at WARN level (or increments a `plan_review.criterion_error{id=asserted-capability}` counter) and the review output annotates the criterion result as ERROR rather than PASS.' Reference whatever error-surfacing mechanism the existing agent-tier criteria (e.g. `removal-rationale`) already use.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the asserted-capability eval fixtures should follow `removal-rationale`'s pattern (added inline to `plan-review-finder.eval.yaml`) rather than a new standalone `plan-review-asserted-capability.eval.yaml`; if a standalone file is intentional, add a rationale distinguishing it from the precedent.
evidence: plan-review-finder.eval.yaml:345-393: all removal-rationale fixtures (5 cases: FP-dead-code, R-error-path, R-intent-marker, R-fabricated, R-e5-overlap) live in the shared finder spec, criterion-labeled
No separate plan-review-removal-rationale.eval.yaml exists in src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/
eval_solver.py:91-115: _run_criterion_case reads a criterion's descriptor and calls pass1_chunk — works for any registered criterion from the shared eval machinery
_llm_eval_commands.py:99-183: rebar criteria eval already runs per-criterion calibration from the shared spec without a dedicated per-criterion YAML
Plan Scope/Files: 'src/rebar/llm/eval_specs/plan-review-asserted-capability.eval.yaml' listed as new file
findings.finding: The plan ships a NEW separate `plan-review-asserted-capability.eval.yaml` eval-spec file (Scope/Files section) plus a committed `asserted_capability_sanity.json` result artifact, but `removal-rationale` (the cited direct structural precedent) added its 5 recall/false-accept fixtures to the EXISTING `plan-review-finder.eval.yaml` (lines 345-393) — not a separate file. The `rebar criteria eval <id>` command already reads criterion-labeled cases from that shared spec and calibrates any registered criterion without a dedicated eval YAML. A new standalone eval YAML file proliferates file surface beyond what the established pattern requires.
scenarios: A separate eval YAML must be maintained alongside the shared finder spec, creating two locations where asserted-capability test cases could drift.
Future criteria authors see two patterns (inline in finder spec vs. standalone YAML) with no canonical guidance on which to follow.
findings.suggested_fix: Add the 5 must-fire/must-not-fire cases as `criterion: asserted-capability` entries in the existing `plan-review-finder.eval.yaml`, mirroring the `removal-rationale` block (lines 345-393). The separate sanity JSON can remain as a committed run artifact without a new eval-spec file.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Harden the sanity-check must-fire fixture cases against future module evolution: either (a) use synthetic/hypothetical module names that don't exist in the real repo (mirroring the E4 gateway.py pattern), (b) include frozen `files:` snapshots of the relevant module stubs in the eval spec cases so the agent reads the fixture's synthetic version rather than the live file, or (c) add an explicit fixture-maintenance note in the eval spec documenting that each must-fire case's validity depends on the named module retaining its current capability gap, and commit a `capability_gap_check` assertion that fails CI if the gap closes.
evidence: Plan: 'reconstructed to their original false-asserting plan text, grounded against current modules' — the grounding is against live modules whose capability surface can change.
Plan: 'The committed result ...shows the probe FIRES on >=2 of 3 misses (report §6 R1 threshold)' — committed as a fixed artifact, but the underlying grounding is mutable.

Contrast: the existing E4 scope-exclusion eval case (R-E4-scope-exclusion-codebase) uses a hypothetical 'gateway.py' that does not exist in the repo, so the agent's grep is consistently empty — the recall case is self-contained and stable.

eval_solver._run_criterion_case: agentic criterion cases run against cfg.repo_path (the live rebar checkout), not a frozen fixture repo — so future code changes are automatically visible to the probe.
findings.finding: The sanity-check recall guarantee (recall>=0.66 committed artifact) is mechanically fragile: the three must-fire fixture cases are grounded against real named rebar modules (completion.py, compact.py, fetcher.py) whose current capability-absence is what makes them fire. If any future story fills the gap (e.g., adds signing to completion.py), the must-fire case silently flips to a no-fire without any fixture-maintenance signal — invalidating the committed recall metric. The plan provides no hardening strategy (synthetic/hypothetical module names, frozen file snapshots via 'files:', or an explicit fixture-maintenance note) to make the recall claim durable.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (bounded sanity check) / Scope (eval fixture set)
scenarios: A future story adds signing to completion.py (resolving dc58's original gap); the dc58-reconstructed must-fire fixture case now passes the probe cleanly, recall drops to 1/3 (<0.66), but the committed artifact still shows recall=0.66 — silent fixture rot.
A reviewer reads the committed sanity JSON as proof-of-effectiveness without realising the current code no longer triggers it.
findings.suggested_fix: In the eval spec cases for dc58/db7b/5886, use synthetic module names (e.g. 'src/rebar/llm/hypothetical_signing_helper.py' or 'src/rebar/llm/completion_v2.py') that will never be created, or include `files:` stubs in the eval case that override the live file content — mirroring how the completion-verifier.eval.yaml injects fixture source files. This makes the recall result module-evolution-immune.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a proof step to AC2 (or a separate AC3 sub-item) that explicitly asserts `validate_criteria_guide()` returns no errors, e.g. `python -c "from rebar.llm.plan_review import registry as r; assert r.validate_criteria_guide()==[]; print('guide OK')"`.
evidence: '`validate_criteria_guide()` (:530) flags a missing section — else... CI fails (the exact failure that bit R2/b080)'
AC2 proof: `assert '## asserted-capability' in open('docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md').read()` — checks section presence only, does not call validate_criteria_guide()
findings.finding: The plan body (What section) identifies `validate_criteria_guide()` as a CI invariant that will fail if the guide section is missing, but no AC explicitly runs or asserts `validate_criteria_guide()` returns clean — AC2 only checks section existence via `'## asserted-capability' in open(...).read()` and `explain_criterion`, which may not exercise all invariants the validator enforces (e.g., required subsection structure, formatting contracts). This leaves a described CI failure mode uncovered by the acceptance criteria.
findings.location: What section ('`validate_criteria_guide()` (:530) flags a missing section') vs. AC2 proof command
scenarios: The guide section is added but its internal structure violates a format contract enforced by validate_criteria_guide(); AC2 passes (section present) but CI fails on the validator — implementer is unwarned.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC2 proof command with: `assert r.validate_criteria_guide()==[]` (or equivalent import path), mirroring the pattern already used for `validate_packaged_routing()==[]` in AC1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` is produced by a generation script/Makefile target; if so, name the script in the Scope and add an AC or implementation note confirming it auto-discovers criteria from `registry.CANONICAL_LLM` (or explicitly calls out updating the script as a required deliverable).
evidence: Plan uses the word '(regenerated)' for this file, strongly implying automated generation rather than hand-editing
The generation script/Makefile target is not mentioned anywhere in the plan
AC2 checks for the section's presence but does not verify the guide can survive a subsequent regeneration
No mention of updating a generator or confirming the generator auto-discovers from registry.CANONICAL_LLM
findings.finding: The plan lists `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md (regenerated)` as a deliverable file but does not mention whether a generation script produces it nor how that script will be updated to include the new `asserted-capability` section. If the guide is auto-generated from a script or Makefile target (likely given the '(regenerated)' label), and that script does not auto-discover new criteria from the registry, the next regeneration run will drop the new section — silently stranding the documentation.
scenarios: Developer runs `make docs` or equivalent after merging; the generation script rewrites the guide without the `## asserted-capability` section because the script was not updated
The stale section is not caught by CI because the stale-doc check only runs on the generated artifact, not on the generator source
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope > Files: the generation script (e.g., `scripts/gen_criteria_guide.py` or equivalent) and confirm in the AC or implementation notes that it reads from `registry.CANONICAL_LLM` so new entries are automatically included — or state explicitly that the guide is hand-maintained (removing the ambiguity introduced by '(regenerated)').

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `workflow_ops.py:279-287` (the CODEBASE_GROUNDED consumer driving Pass-2 agentic verification) in the Scope/Files section or a comment confirming its auto-pickup is the intended Pass-2 wiring for this criterion.
evidence: Plan AC: `assert 'asserted-capability' in r.CODEBASE_GROUNDED`
workflow_ops.py:279: '(E4/G1G2/A1/G6)? This is the boolean the dynamic Pass-2 verify branch reads'
workflow_ops.py:287: `code_grounded = any finding cites a CODEBASE_GROUNDED criterion`
No mention of workflow_ops.py in the plan's Scope or Files sections
findings.finding: workflow_ops.py (which reads CODEBASE_GROUNDED to gate Pass-2 agentic verification) is an unenumerated consumer of the CODEBASE_GROUNDED frozenset expansion, but the plan's Scope section and Files list do not mention it.
scenarios: Adding asserted-capability to CODEBASE_GROUNDED means Pass-2's agentic-verification branch fires on this criterion's findings — this is the INTENDED behavior per the plan's 'Pass-2 verifier grades its two criterion-local binary sub-answers', but the consumer is undocumented, so a reviewer of the PR cannot confirm the pass-2 wiring is intentional without tracing it themselves.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the Mechanism section: 'Because `asserted-capability` is added to `CODEBASE_GROUNDED`, `workflow_ops.py`'s Pass-2 agentic-branch trigger will automatically include findings from this criterion — no change to `workflow_ops.py` required.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether the `.eval.yaml` fixture cases will carry `files:` blocks reproducing the relevant module stubs (e.g. a stub `src/rebar/llm/completion.py`, `_commands/compact.py`, `fetcher.py`) so the AGENT finder can ground capability claims during `rebar criteria eval`, or explicitly document how the eval path provides module access (e.g. running against the live repo_root rather than a disposable fixture).
evidence: AC-4: 'grounded against current modules' and 'raw `rebar criteria eval asserted-capability --output json` per-case output preserved'
eval_solver._run_criterion_case: passes only inline `input` text; calls `pass1_chunk(..., agentic=True)` with no fixture repo files unless the case carries a `files:` block via `case_store`
eval_solver.case_store: yields a repo_root with only `_git_init` + `_write_files(case.get('files'))` — no production source tree
The plan names no `files:` blocks in the fixture spec, and does not address how the agentic finder reaches the named modules (completion.py, _commands/compact.py, fetcher.py)
findings.finding: The plan's AC-4 sanity-check asserts that `rebar criteria eval asserted-capability` can fire on the 3 E1-verified misses 'grounded against current modules', but the `_run_criterion_case` eval path runs the AGENT finder against inline `input` text with a near-empty fixture repo (only a README). Unless the `.eval.yaml` fixture cases include `files:` blocks reproducing the relevant module stubs (e.g. completion.py, _commands/compact.py, fetcher.py), the agentic grep/resolve_symbol calls will find nothing to ground against — making the 'grounded against current modules' assertion unverifiable by the eval tooling as specified.
scenarios: Agent runs `rebar criteria eval asserted-capability` on the dc58 miss-fire fixture; its grep for `rebar/llm/completion.py` finds nothing in the disposable repo; it cannot confirm or refute the capability claim; the probe silently passes (false no-fire), making the AC-4 pass condition of recall>=0.66 unachievable.
Maintainer later adds the criterion but cannot validate the eval fixture works as described, creating a misleading committed result artifact.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add `files:` entries to each must-fire fixture case in the eval.yaml that reproduce the minimum module stub needed (the function signatures the plan asserts are absent/present), or (b) add a note to AC-4 that the eval runs with `repo_root` pointing to the actual rebar repo (via `case_store` seeded from the real tree), and confirm `calibrate_criterion` supports passing a live `repo_root` through the solver (it does, per `_criterion_solver`).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
